title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption:
Life expectancy, including UN projections
The period life expectancy at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.

Across the timeline from 1950 to 2100, the chart plots period life expectancy at birth in years for five distinct series: Japan, Sweden, Brazil, India, and the World average. The vertical axis measures age in years, ranging from 0 to 100, while the horizontal axis spans the years 1950 through 2100. Solid lines represent observed historical data from 1950 to roughly 2023, while dotted lines indicate medium-variant projections for the future. In 1950, the data points show a wide spread: Sweden starts highest at approximately 70 years, while India starts lowest at roughly 41 years, with the World average near 48 years. By 2020, the lines show significant convergence and upward movement. Japan rises from around 60 years in 1950 to over 80 years by 2020, surpassing Sweden. India shows a steep increase from the bottom of the range to approximately 70 years. A sharp, temporary drop is visible in the India and World lines around 2020, followed by a resumption of the upward trend in the projections. Looking toward 2100, the dotted lines show all series continuing to rise. Japan is projected to reach the highest value, approaching 95 years. Sweden follows closely, projected to exceed 90 years. Brazil and India are projected to converge near the 85-year mark, while the World average is projected to reach just above 80 years. The visual pattern displays a consistent long-term increase in life expectancy across all tracked regions, with the gap between the highest and lowest values narrowing over the century.
